splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on when actuarial assumptions must be finalized for withdrawal liability calculations under ERISA. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be selected before the statutory measurement date (end of prior plan year), providing employers greater predictability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that a plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year and raises questions about judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is tolled during periods of absconding, allowing courts to revoke supervised release even after the original term would have expired.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is not tolled during absconding because no statute expressly authorizes tolling, and judicial creation of such a doctrine exceeds courts' authority.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: A longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split exists on whether federal prisoners face the stricter Section 2244(b)(1) gatekeeping requirement — categorically barring previously presented claims — in addition to Section 2255(h)'s requirements. The outcome determines whether federal prisoners must clear an additional hurdle when bringing successive collateral attacks on their convictions.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims under § 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement beyond § 2255(h).

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar does not apply to § 2255 motions, so federal prisoners need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures, specifically ballot-counting deadlines. The outcome could reshape how courts evaluate pre-election challenges nationwide, affecting rules on mail-in voting, ballot counting deadlines, and other election administration practices.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates have Article III standing to challenge election rules pre-election because vote-dilution or competitive injury from allegedly unlawful ballot-counting procedures is sufficient injury-in-fact.

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates lack Article III standing for pre-election challenges to ballot-counting procedures because the alleged injury is too speculative or generalized before the election occurs.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to agency interpretations (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, including Sentencing Guidelines.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity trumps agency deference when construing ambiguous criminal provisions. At least three circuits hold lenity prevails, while the Third Circuit panel held that agency deference trumps lenity. This tension has grown more significant after Loper Bright overruled Chevron deference.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to agency interpretations in the criminal context, preventing agencies from using delegated power to expand criminal liability.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency deference (Auer/Kisor) takes precedence over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous sentencing guidelines or criminal provisions, allowing anti-defendant readings supported by agency commentary.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether 'and' means 'and' or 'or' in the safety-valve provision. The Ninth and Eleventh Circuits read it conjunctively (all three criteria required), while the Fifth, Sixth, Seventh, and Eighth Circuits read it disjunctively (any one criterion disqualifies). This affects whether defendants can avoid mandatory minimum sentences.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or' based on statutory context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines commentary (Application Note 1) that includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy within the definition of 'controlled substance offense' under U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) is entitled to deference when the guideline text does not mention inchoate offenses.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1's expansion of 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy, aiding and abetting, and attempt is a valid interpretation of the guideline text or impermissibly expands its scope. This affects career-offender sentencing enhancements for many federal defendants with prior inchoate drug convictions.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of an ambiguous guideline and is entitled to controlling weight under Stinson/Kisor, so inchoate offenses qualify as controlled substance offenses.

positions.label: Commentary exceeds guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and the commentary impermissibly expands the definition, so inchoate offenses do not qualify as controlled substance offenses for career-offender purposes.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) may be considered as a factor supporting 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law — alone or in combination with other factors — when evaluating compassionate release motions. Some circuits categorically bar such consideration, while others allow it as one factor. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 policy statement attempted to resolve this by allowing it, but circuits disagree on whether that statement is binding.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered as one factor among others in evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, whether alone or in combination with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state-controlled substance is not listed on the federal drug schedules.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) requires the substance to be federally controlled or whether any state-law drug offense qualifies. The Eighth Circuit holds there is no federal-law limitation, while other circuits disagree, creating disparate sentencing outcomes for defendants with identical state drug convictions.

positions.label: State-only substances qualify
positions.summary: Holds that the definition of 'controlled substance offense' under § 4B1.2(b) includes any offense under state law, even if the substance is not controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act, because there is no textual basis to graft a federal-law limitation.

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the underlying substance is also controlled under federal law, applying a categorical approach that requires overlap between state and federal schedules.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value between a Chapter 13 filing and a good-faith conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the estate or the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held it belongs to the estate, creating a split with circuits that hold such appreciation inures to the debtor. This affects debtors' incentives to convert and creditor recoveries.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), coupled with § 541(a)'s broad definition of estate property, means post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that under the Bankruptcy Code's structure and § 348(f)(1)(A)'s purpose of protecting good-faith converting debtors, post-petition pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtors, not the estate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners challenging the conditions or terms of their confinement (as opposed to fact or duration) must proceed via habeas corpus or may bring claims under Bivens or other civil rights statutes.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive federal remedy for prisoners challenging conditions of confinement or whether such challenges may proceed as civil rights actions. This affects the procedural requirements and remedies available to prisoners, particularly federal prisoners challenging BOP decisions about prerelease custody under the First Step Act.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive for confinement challenges
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to the terms and conditions of confinement that affect duration of imprisonment must be brought via habeas corpus, not civil rights actions.

positions.label: Civil rights actions also available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring civil rights actions (Bivens or § 1983) to challenge conditions of confinement, and habeas is not the exclusive remedy for such claims.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release at revocation sentencing, or whether revocation sentences must be based solely on the breach of trust.
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit allows district courts to expressly punish defendants for supervised release violations, placing it at the extreme of a circuit split. Other circuits hold that revocation sentences should be based on the breach of the court's trust, not as direct punishment for the underlying violation conduct. This affects hundreds of revocation proceedings annually.

positions.label: Punishment for violation permitted
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may impose revocation sentences that expressly punish defendants for their supervised release violations, not just for the breach of judicial trust.

positions.label: Revocation based on breach of trust only
positions.summary: Holds that the purpose of revocation sentencing is to sanction the breach of the defendant's relationship with the court, and revocation sentences should not be used as direct punishment for the underlying violation.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil appeal deadline (60 days for government party) or criminal appeal deadline (14 days) applies to appeals from orders on Hyde Amendment motions for attorney's fees in criminal cases.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (triggering civil appeal deadlines) or part of the criminal case (triggering shorter criminal deadlines). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies, but some circuits have held otherwise.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the civil appeal deadline under Rule 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion arises from and is part of the criminal case, so the shorter criminal appeal deadline under Rule 4(b) applies.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Trident Seafoods Corp.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal medical records in litigation is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine as an order affecting a value of 'high order.'
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order' warranting immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine, but other circuits have taken varying approaches to immediate appealability of sealing denials, creating uncertainty about when such orders are reviewable before final judgment.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denials of motions to seal medical records are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order.'

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal are not immediately appealable because the privacy interest can be adequately vindicated on appeal from a final judgment or through other means such as mandamus.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FAAAA's broad preemption clause extends to state-law tort claims against freight brokers for negligently arranging transportation, or whether such claims fall outside the statute's preemptive scope. This affects the ability of injured parties to seek compensation from brokers when motor carriers cause harm.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are 'related to' the broker's services and therefore preempted by the FAAAA's broad preemption provision.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted because they do not regulate the broker's prices, routes, or services within the meaning of the FAAAA.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), which prohibits unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen, circuits have split on whether the longstanding prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances survives Second Amendment scrutiny when applied to marijuana users. Some circuits have upheld the statute while others have found it unconstitutional as applied.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) is consistent with the historical tradition of firearm regulation and does not violate the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons who use intoxicating substances.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release motions can serve as vehicles for claims that overlap with habeas corpus challenges under § 2255. Some circuits allow consideration of legal errors as one factor in compassionate release, while others hold that such claims must be channeled through § 2255 proceedings.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider factors related to potential errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Must use § 2255 for conviction/sentence challenges
positions.summary: Holds that claims challenging the legality of a conviction or sentence must be brought through § 2255 and cannot be repackaged as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FIFRA's labeling requirements preempt state tort claims alleging that a pesticide manufacturer should have included additional warnings on its label. This affects products liability litigation involving herbicides and pesticides nationwide, including high-profile Roundup litigation.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because allowing such claims would impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those required under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by FIFRA because they parallel, rather than impose requirements 'in addition to or different from,' federal labeling requirements.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of the right to make and enforce contracts?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standard for § 1981 claims in hospitality and service contexts, particularly regarding what constitutes interference with the right to make and enforce contracts and the available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach against other circuits' frameworks.

positions.label: Narrower standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 claims require a showing of interference with an actual contractual relationship or attempted contract, applying a more restrictive view of what constitutes actionable discrimination.

positions.label: Broader standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 covers a broader range of discriminatory conduct in business establishments, including degraded service or hostile treatment that impairs the enjoyment of contracted services.
